title: Which Jim Carrey Character Lives Inside Your Head?
dek: From rubber-faced chaos to heartbroken quiet — answer honestly and we'll tell you exactly which unhinged, iconic, or oddly tender Carrey role is basically your personality.

quiz.intro: No wrong answers here — just go with whatever your gut screams first.

questions.question: A total stranger starts talking to you at a party. What's your instinct?

answers.text: Turn it into a bit immediately — accents, sound effects, all of it.
I get weirdly, unnaturally confident for about ten minutes.
Smile, nod, secretly wonder if they're an actor hired to talk to me.
I become a completely different person than the one who walked in.

questions.question: Your day just fell apart completely. How do you cope?

answers.text: Unleash the chaos I've been bottling up all week.
Assume it's fake and someone is testing me.
Turn my breakdown into a legendary bit no one will forget.
Blame a higher power and demand better service.

questions.question: How do you handle being the center of attention?

answers.text: Uncomfortable — what if everyone's actually watching me?
I'll push it until the crowd doesn't know if I'm joking.
I want it AND I want it to mean something important.
I want to be noticed for how clever I am, specifically.

questions.question: Pick the breakup style that sounds most like you.

answers.text: I turn the heartbreak into a legendary performance for everyone else.
I demand the universe explain itself to me.
I plot, I scheme, I need to understand exactly why.
I don't handle it well and I definitely don't let go easily.

questions.question: What's your relationship with rules and authority?

answers.text: I'd like to BE the authority, honestly.
I outsmart the authority and leave a clue about it.
I bend them slightly to get closer to people.
I mostly just want to be left quietly alone.

questions.question: Your friends would describe your energy as...

answers.text: Sharp, theatrical, a bit obsessive about being clever.
Intense — I come on strong and don't always read the room.
Quiet, introverted, the one who overthinks everything.
Unhinged, but somehow always right in the end.

questions.question: How do you actually feel about being watched or perceived?

answers.text: I want to be perceived specifically by the people I care about.
I'd rather not be perceived at all, if I'm honest.
Doesn't bother me — I'm basically always performing anyway.
I hide my wildest self until absolutely necessary.

questions.question: If you had godlike power for one week, what's the first thing you'd do?

answers.text: Undo one specific memory that's been haunting me.
Use it to solve a genuinely absurd case.
Cause chaos, then feel guilty, then cause more chaos.
Test if the whole world around me is actually staged.

questions.question: What's your biggest flaw, if you're being honest?

answers.text: I take everything way too far, way too fast.
I let out my worst impulses and blame 'the other side of me.'
I'm too trusting of a world that might not be what it seems.
I blur the line between who I am and who I'm performing as.

questions.question: Last one: pick the ending that feels the most 'you.'

answers.text: Finally learning to control both sides of myself.
Walking out the door into the unknown real world.
Taking a final, unforgettable bow for the people who loved me.
Realizing the point was never the power, it was the people.

results.name: Ace Ventura
results.subtitle: Ace Ventura: Pet Detective (1994)
results.description: You're chaos with a purpose — loud, physical, completely unfiltered, and somehow always right underneath all the noise. This was the role that turned Jim Carrey into an overnight superstar and set the template for the rubber-faced mania he'd spend the rest of the '90s riffing on.

results.name: Stanley Ipkiss / The Mask
results.subtitle: The Mask (1994)
results.description: You've got a whole other side of yourself you keep locked down until something sets it loose — and then it's cartoon-level chaos. Carrey's dual performance as the meek Stanley and his uninhibited green-faced alter ego let him show off both the manic energy and the underlying sweetness that made him a star.

results.name: Truman Burbank
results.subtitle: The Truman Show (1998)
results.description: Sweet, trusting, and a little too comfortable with the world as it's been presented to you — until the cracks start showing. This was Carrey's big pivot toward drama, and it earned him a Golden Globe for a performance that's quietly one of the saddest and most hopeful of his career.

results.name: Andy Kaufman
results.subtitle: Man on the Moon (1999)
results.description: Nobody can tell where the performance ends and you begin, and honestly, you like it that way. Carrey's method-acted transformation into the late comedian was so committed that it became its own documentary years later, and it won him a second consecutive Golden Globe even though the film itself struggled at the box office.

results.name: Bruce Nolan
results.subtitle: Bruce Almighty (2003)
results.description: You want the power, you want the spotlight, and you're absolutely going to abuse both before you learn your lesson. Reviews were mixed on the film overall, but critics agreed Carrey's manic energy was what kept the whole thing afloat, and it went on to become one of his biggest box-office hits ever.

results.name: The Riddler / Edward Nygma
results.subtitle: Batman Forever (1995)
results.description: You need everyone to know exactly how clever you are, and you'll leave a trail of clues to prove it even if it costs you everything. Carrey went full theatrical menace in the neon-green suit, reportedly improvising several of his most memorable lines, and stole scenes from a cast that included Val Kilmer and Tommy Lee Jones.

results.name: The Cable Guy
results.subtitle: The Cable Guy (1996)
results.description: You come on strong, you attach fast, and you genuinely don't notice when it's too much for the other person. Audiences in 1996 were so thrown by Carrey playing an unsettling, obsessive character instead of a lovable goof that the film was branded a flop — only to be reassessed decades later as a cult classic.

results.name: Joel Barish
results.subtitle: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004)
results.description: You're quiet, introverted, and you'd honestly rather erase the pain than sit with it — until you realize the memory might be worth keeping after all. This was Carrey playing almost entirely against type as a bookish, reserved heartbroken guy, in what's widely considered one of the best performances of his career.
